VerbalDescription: Adjust the height of your grid to 1. The width can remain the same. For each shape that is in the test input grid, make one square yellow. Leave the rest of the squares black.

Make a grid of one row in height by 6 columns i.e. have one row of 6 cells.  Count the number of individual shapes in the original image and colour each of the blocks in your new rwo yellow depending on the number of shapes i.e. one shape means colour in one of the 6 cells yellow (starting from left to right), 3 shapes means colour in 3 cells from left to right yellow, 6 shapes would mean colour in all 6 cells yellow

Make the answer grid 1(height) 6 (width) and colour it black. Count the number of objects in the demonstration, that is how many squares of the answer grid should be in yellow. The yellow blocks should be added from left to right.
Make the gird 1 high by 6 long and fill the gird in yellow by one square for each object present, starting from the left.
Amount of objects equate to number of yellow squares in the new grid 1x6

create boxes 1 by 6

As there is one item you will need to pick the yellow colour and colour how many items there are in the diagram

Your grid will be one square high and six squares wide. You will count the number of objects in your test. This gives you the number of square you must fill in. Starting from left to right, colour in the square or squares to match the number of objects. Note each block you fill in, in the squares, must be yellow in colour.

Make your grid one square high and six squares wide. You need to fill in the empty spaces from left to right. The number of squares needed to be filled is simply the total number of objects in the test image given to you. Ensure that the squares you fill in are coloured yellow.
Use a height of 1 and a width of six. Using yellow and starting from the left, fill in the squares with the total number of objects in the input grid example.
Change the grid to 6x1.  Count how many shapes are on the input grid.  Fill in that number of boxes starting from the left.
Create a grid that is 1 high and 6 wide. Count how many different shapes appear on the original grid. Use this number to fill in that amount of squares, from left to right, with the color yellow.

The rule for this task is reduce the grid to 1x6 and convert the color for each square to yellow. The number of squares that should be yellow should equate to the number of shapes in the original grid.

You will need to create a grid that is one square high and six squares long.  You need to count the shapes that are in the original grid.  You then need to fill out a yellow square starting from the left of the new grid and going to the right.  The number of yellow squares you fill out will correspond to the number of shapes in the original grid.  If there are 3 shapes, then fill out 3 yellow squares, if there are five shapes, then fill out 5 yellow squares, etc.
Resize your grid to 1 height and 6 width. Count the objects in the test grid. Color that number of spaces in your grid yellow, starting from the right, and leave the remaining spaces black.
color the number of boxes
Use a 1x6 grid no matter what. Count how many distinct shapes there are, starting from the left most side of the 1x6 grid, fill each square in the grid for how many total shapes there are.

Count the number of shapes in the input grid and make note of that number. Create a 1x6 grid in which the number of shapes will be represented by yellow squares that you will enter consecutively from left to right corresponding to the number of shapes you noted. Select the same number of yellow squares in the manner described above as the number of shapes that you noted.

Make the grid size 1 height and 6 width. Count how many object there are, as there is three objects you are going to colour 3 boxes yellow starting at the far left and working directly along leaving no changes in between. The other three boxes on the right side should be black.

Change the grid size to 1 height and 6 width. However many shapes there are in the input will determine how many of the squares to colour yellow (e.g. 4 shapes mean you will colour 4 out of 6 squares yellow). Colour the blocks yellow starting from the left like a loading bar
Reduce the grid to 1 x 6, then add yellow squares equal to the number of shapes linearly, starting from the left-hand side.
Reset the grid to a height of 1 and a length of however many rows are in the middle row, or 2nd smallest row, of the pyramid shape.

Count the number of lines in the input object. Look at the object as a pyramid and count from peak to base. then create a output grid of a single line as wide as the number colored levels in the input. Leave uncolored.
Look at how many individual rows of the coloured cells appear. Resize the grid to the following: height - 1 cell, width - number of rows determined above. Colour the grid black.

The amount of rows/columns of coloured blocks must be transformed into the new grid as a series of horizontal black boxes, just one block high. For instance, if there are 4 different green rows/columns depending on how the shape is placed, there would be 4 black squares in length in the new grid. For solid shapes take only the dimensions of a pyramid/sidewards pyramid in height for the new block length, you're looking for straight lines.
Change the input size to the size of the middle size line in the task (1x4). Don't change the colour from black, leave it as is.
Remove inner shape, resize your grid to 1 single grid line with number of width same as number of height your target shape has

The rule is to make a horizontal grid the same size as the centermost colored row or column from the original grid. In this case, it would be a height of 1 and a width of 2, since there are 2 orange squares in the original shape. Make sure the squares are both black.

Make the height of the grid 1. Make the grid width the size of the height of the original shape.
The height of the original shape should just be counted using the coloured squares. Please discount any black squares from your height. Leave it all black.

Lines are shapes that have a thickness of one cell (1 by 1, 1 by 3, 1 by 6, etc.). Count the number of line shapes that are in the input grid. The lines may or may not be directly next to each other, and will be generally small to large. set the grid height to 1, and the grid width to the same number as the number of lines. Click 'Resize' and submit.

Resize the grid to 1 times by the number of coloured rows or columns. Columns if the longest side of the pattern is vertical/ rows if the longest side is horizontal. Keep the resulting square(s) black.
its  done on how many steps up the object goes, so for demonstration one it goes up 4 times, demonstration 2 3 for example and its counted by black squares for the answer
Count the sides of the target shape, then make a one line grid with as many squares as sides in the target shape. Color the squares orange.
past the all box
Look at the shape shown. Note the number of sides shown. E.g Triangle is 3, square 4. Create a grid that is this number wide, and 1 block high. Then, flood fill the grid orange
Make the grid highest 1 and width the number of vertices on the shape. Then fill it in orange
Use the grid size of the number of size represented by the geometric shape and fill with an orange color.
Number of sides equal new grid width
Count the number of edges on the shape, then create a grid with a height of 1 and the width is the number of edges. Fill this grid in with orange
Grid size to be height 1 and length equal to number of sides of shape seen in input. Flash fill orange

Count the number of sides of the object e.g. if it is a square it has four sides, three if a triangle. Then make your grid one square high and the number of sides of your object wide. You will then need to colour your object orange. Your object should be a line of blocks, in orange, with the number of blocks equal to the sides of the object in your test image.
Count the number of sides that the shape has. Make a 1 x that number grid (like a horizontal line) and paint it orange.
symbol side calculate by number and provide number of box

You will see a shape. Count the sides of the shape. For example, 4 sides of a square, 3 for a triangle. Then, change the dimension of the answer to be one block high times however many sides your shape had. So, for a square the grid would be 1 X 4. Then color the single row orange.
Count the number of corners on the blue shape. This is the width of the grid. The grid is one square high. Color the entire grid orange.
count how many sides the shape has and that is how many squares long the shape is, it must be the colour orange.
Height is 1, width is the amount of sides the shape has. Colour is orange
Count the corners of the shape. Using orange cells only & the same amount of cells as there is corners. Create a horizontal line. Resize the grid, so only the orange cells fit within.
change the grid size to however many sides there are on the shape horizontally then fill in orange
There will be one shape in the input. Count the sides of the shape. Make a grid as long as the number of sides, one block high. Color the grid orange.

Make a grid with a height of one square.  The width of the grid should be equal to the number of sides of the blue shape in the test input.  For example if the shape is a hexagon, the width should be 6 squares long.  All the squares in the grid should be orange.

You need to count the number of sides of the shape in the grid.  You will need to create a new grid that is one square high.  The width will be the number of sides that the shape had.  You must color the new grid fully orange.

Use the same grid size as the size of the biggest object in the input. In your answers, at every intersection where blocks overlap, the vertical color block must overlap the horizontal color block. Keep all colors the same except the overlapping block which must have the same color as the vertical blocks
do a grid size 1x8 so it is just a long rectangle. colour block the whole object orange.
Create a 1 high grid, with the width of the grid being the same as the number of sides of the object on input. Then, colour this grid orange.

The test image contains an identifiable shape. Count the number of sides in the shape, then resize your grid so it is 1 square high, and the length of the grid is equal to the number of sides in the shape in the test image. Color each square of your grid orange.
Count the number of sides (lets call it N) that of the shape in the input. Size a grid which is height=1 and width=N. Color the whole thing orange.
Count the number of individual shapes. Then change the grid height to 1, and the width to the same number as the number of shapes. Color the resulting grid completely green
count how many separate 'snake like' shapes there are and that is how long the answer is and colour this green.
Count the lines and resize the grid to that length and make it green
Count the number of colors on the grid. The grid size of the solution is 1x(the number of colors) on the first grid. Then, fill the new grid with green.
Count how many non-black shapes are in the image. Use this number as length of your output grid. The height of the grid is one. Colour - green
Count the number of shapes in your input (lets say N). create grid where height=1, width=N. Color them green
Count the number of discrete shapes (n) within the grid. Construct the answer using a grid size of (n) x 1 and colour it green.

Count how many shapes are in the task grid
now mine had just one so i made a grid Hight 1 x Width x1
Now colour green.
The solution is a single grid square. Change Grid size to 1x1. Change color to green.

Count the number of unique colors in the original image.  Your final image should simply be a single row of black squares and the number of squares should equal the number of unique colors in the original image.
Use a grid size that corresponds to the number of different colors in the input for your width. The color will be different than the shapes from the input.
Identify how many different colours there are in your image (i.e., just yellow = 1, yellow and green = 2), then you need to make the width of your image that size, the height will be 1.
Count the number of colours in the input. Now resize the grid so that the height is 1 and the width is the amount of colours there were in the input.
Make a single horizontal line as the grid, the length of which is dependent on how many different colours are in the original image. Four colours means a horizontal line four squares long.
Resize height to one and width to the number of different colour shapes there are (ignore black). Once resized, leave this shape black.
Count the number of unique colors used in the input (not including black). Set the output height to 1, and the width to the number of unique colors. Fill the entire area with black.
Count the number of different (non-black) colours in the input. The output should be a black grid of height 1, and length equal to the number of colours counted.
Determine the number of colours within the grid. Reshape the grid Y axis to the number of colours to form a black line of a specific length.

Count how many different colours are shown in the picture (excluding black). Then, create a grid that is this number wide, and 1 block tall. Make sure the grid is filled in black, then this is the solution.
make a grid of black background spaces only that's just as wide as the smallest connected group of black background spaces in the original image.
Count the amount of different colors in the original grid. Use a grid of height:1 and length equal to the amount of colors. Leave it black.
set the grid size in the following format H=1, w=x with x being the number of different colours In the example
examine how many colors you have in the input grid, and adjust your grid number to that. for example if you have 2 colors, adjust your grid to give you just 2 boxes
use the same grid as in the input. There will be multiple objects in the input; in your answer, create a grid with the number of colors that appear in the input
used to represent a diffrent color appearing in a box apart from black
Create one tile for every colour in the picture (excluding black). For example, if there are 2 colours then the grid should be 1x2
Count the number of colors and make a blank grid based on that. Height will be 1 and width will be the number of colors.

I guessed; my best guess was that the solution would be made only of black squares and the number of squares would be determined by finding the numbers for the heighth and width, and subtracting the smaller of the two from the larger.

You must count the number of original colors in the grid.  If two separate shapes have the same color, this will only count as one color.  Once you have counted the number of original colors in the grid, you will then need to create a new grid that is one square high.  The width of the grid will be the amount of original colors.  The new grid will be colored black.
Use the same grid size as the input. There will be more than one coloured object in the input, in your answer, agjust the grid to the amount of colour observed ( keep its colour the same).
Determine the amount of colours that are present in the input. Adjust the height to 1, and adjust the width accordingly to the amount of colours present in the input.
Use the amount of black squares inside the larger square and re-arrange them so they form a horizontal line.
There will be a green shape in the input. Count how many black squares are inside the green shape. Use this number to make a black horizontal output of x across

The colour changes to black (the centre blocks inside the colour) The grid size changes to the amount of the black boxes inside the coloured image and the height stays at 1 to create a line of black boxes.
Identify the number of black squares housed in the middle of the shape shown. Then create a grid that has a height of 1 and a width of the number of squares you counted. Leave it black.

Determine how many black cells or empty space are inside of the shape given. While leaving the HEIGHT at 1 resize the grid so that the WIDTH reflects the amount of black cells / empty space are inside if the object/shape.
count how many black squares are inside the coloured object and set the grid in a line so if theres 6 black squares inside the green square then you would have a line of 6 black squares
Resize the grid to match the opening in the feature.
For this task i just made the grid 1 height and the same width as the amount of blank squares inside the shape.
Count the squares inside each shape. Using only a grid one square high, make the width as large as this number
count the empty squares within the shape (?), then resize the grid as 1 vertically and horizontally will be the number of shapes you counted (?)
There should be an outline shape to start with. Count how many black boxes are in the middle. Resize the grid to 1 high, and however many middle black boxes there are is the width.
Count the number of no color squares inside the rectangular color item in the input box. Resize the grid to 1 high by the number of those inner no color squares wide.
Look at the green shape. Look at the black squares in the middle of the green shape and count them (number = X). Then, create a black grid that is 1 block high and X number of blocks wide

The amount of black squares within the green object will be the size of the new grid, all squares must be laid out horizontally so that the total size of the grid is one block high and the amount of black squares in length
Count how many black squares there are inside the hole. This is how wide you should make your canvas as 1 height. It should be coloured black.
Make a horizontal line based on how many black spaces are in the middle of the rectangle. If there are 6 spaces in the middle, make a horizontal black line six spaces long.
Count up the squares surrounded by the outside colour, and resize an empty canvas to 1 high, that many wide.
Look at the number of black cells inside the shape. Count them. Now make one row with the same number of black cells you saw in the shape. Leave the row completely black.
Change the grid to 7x1 and, starting from the top, add the amount of single cells that you see in the original. Make them yellow.
Select correct number of squares. Draw one objects. Draw yellow object on top that is same number of squares that are colored in the object.
Set grid to 7 high by 1 wide. Fill a square in the grid starting from the top in yellow for each coloured single square located in the test input.

Resize your grid to 7 height and 1 width. In the test grid there is a rectangle of a solid color. Count the spaces inside that rectangle that are different colors. In your grid, starting from the top, color that number of spaces yellow, leaving the remaining spaces black.
You're presented with a square frame and random squares filled within the frame. On a 7 x 1 grid, fill in however many squares within the frame in yellow
Make a bar 7x1 and  count the number of squares in colour inside the frame. On your bar colour in that number of squares yellow (from the top)

I think the solution to this is the same as a previous task. Just identify the larger, un-filled shape, along with the spots of differing colour within this larger shape. Count the amount of spots that we have within the larger shape.

Next, change the width of the grid that we have to 1, and its height to 7. By using the amount of the spots that we have identified, from the top, colour the exact number of squares in the grid. The squares must be in yellow.

This looks harder than it is. All you need is a box with height 7 and width one. Count the number on squares contained inside the box in the prompt. Colour in that same number of squares yellow starting from the top and going down, and leave the rest black.
1 column, 7 black rows. Count number of coloured blocks inside task and fill black blocks with yellow from top down.
change the grid size to 1 x 7 then colour the number of squares yellow starting from the top that matches the number of non-black cubes within the coloured square
The examples showed that the yellow square in the answers were defined by how many cells were coloured on the inside of the box.
Count the number of squares inside the coloured boundaries that are a different colour. Create a 1x7 grid and fill in the number of coloured squares that you counted in yellow starting at the top.
Make your grid's height to 7 and width to 1. On the input there's a box with dots inside. Count how many dots are there. Put how many dots are there starting at the top and make the color yellow.

Grid size height 7, width 1. Make the image black. Count the number of colored squares that are inside the rectangle - call this X. Fill in X number of yellow squares from top. Leave the bottom part black / blank.

Make a grid that is 7 high and 1 wide. Count the number of colored squares within the colored rectangle in the original. Now color the top x squares of the new grid yellow, where x is equal to that number of colored squares within the rectangle of the original.

Within the sample grid, there is a border around a certain number of colored squares. Determine how many colored squares are within that border. When you know the correct number of squares within that border, create a 7x1 grid and select that same number of squares starting with the highest square in the grid but use the color yellow only. The number of yellow squares should match the number of squares determined from the sample and should be immediately adjacent to each other starting from the top of the grid.

Create a grid that is one cell wide and seven cells high.  The look at the original object and count the number of colored squares inside the frame or shape.  However many colored squares you will then color your grid starting from the top and using yellow.  So if your original object has three blue squares inside the shape frame your new grid with have three yellow squares at the top and  four black squares at the bottom.

Make a grid with a height of 7 and a width of 1. Count the colored dots inside the object. Use that number to fill with yellow starting from the top (if you have 2 green squares for example inside the object then fill the first 2 squares from the top with yellow).

Change the grid size to 1 width, and a height equal to the number of oddly coloured cubes (cubes differing from the overall colour of the shape). Fill this grid (looks more of a line) with red squares.

Look at the input grid, there will be a border shape of two colours. Take note of the colour that appears the least in this border and the amount of these blocks there are. The answer is a grid that is the same height as the number of the least frequent of the two colour blocks in the input and the width is one block. The colour of this single width column grid must be red.
Find the number of blocks that are a different color.  Count them up and use that number as the height with one as the width and then fill that grid in with red.
Count the number of odd color blocks, and set the solution only as a tower of the number of odd number blocks, but use a dark orange color.

The rule is that you count how many out of place squares there are in the object (in this case blue), and then make your grid a height of that many (in this case 2) with a width of 1. You are going to ignore the original color blue and make the squares red.

You will enconter a grid with a square filled with two colors.  You need to count however many of the lesser color there is.  You will need to create a new grid with a width of one and the height is the amount of squares of the lesser color.  You will color the new grid fully orange.
There will be a shape in the input with several squares that have different colours. Your answer will be a vertical line made up of however many different coloured squares there are in the input
The solution is to create a grid of the total number if colour that was used the least in the input. In this case a 1x1 grid needs to be created. Fill with the colour red
Make a grid that is a single column and fill it with red squares depending on the number of differently coloured squares in the original rectangle.
Count the least common color in circle. Resize width of grid to 1 and the height to as many uncommon colors squares there is. Then color it in bright red.
Count the number of randomly colored squares in the big box. Make a single vertical column grid with the same number of squares and flood fill them red.

Count the colored squares that are a different color from the majority color in the test grid, not including the black squares. Make the grid height the same as the number of squares you counted and make the grid width one square. Color the whole grid light red.
Resize the grid width to 1.  For the height count the number of different coloured squares within the main shape of the image.  Colour all these in red.

Ignoring the majority colours inside the coloured square/rectangle, the individual colours must be counted and placed into a 1 width grid, compiling the height of the blocks by the amount of minority coloured squares. For example, a pink, green and orange block inside a larger yellow square would lead to a 3 block high,1 block wide red grid.  These squares in the new grid must be coloured red.
make an orange tower based on the amount of dots within the shape.

Take the number of squares coloured differently within the shape. Then create a grid that is 1 square wide and however many different coloured squares tall. Then colour all squares in the solution grid bright red.
by quick decision making.

There should be a larger colored shape with several boxes that are colored differently - count those. Resize the grid to be 1 wide, and however many you counted for the height. Then color the whole grid red.
Answer is red. Make grid just as large as the answer. Width is one. Height is equal to the number of squares in the larger shape of the non dominant color.
change grid size to match the number of the colours with the lowest frequency. change colour to red

Count the distance between the coloured squares horizontally or vertically only. Ignore any squares set at diagonals from each other. Do not include the coloured squares themselves, only the gap. If there are multiple coloured squares horizontal or diagonal, count the gaps between all of them along the same line. If there are multiple horizontal and vertical counts you can do, then only count the gaps between the same colours providing they are not vertical. Then, set your input grid to the height of however many gaps you've counted, and a width of 1. Colour all squares red.

Identify loose individual squares - those that differ from the dominant colours of the image. Count them. Create a grid where the height matches the number of aftermentioned squares. The width is one square. Use colour red

There was one object on the input screen which is a different color to the rest. Keep the color the same and reduce the grid size to 1x1. If you have multiple colored square add them all in a single row.

Count the number of individually colored squares that are different than the rest.  Change the height of the grid to that number and the width to 1.  Then change the color of all the remaining squares to red.

You will see a number of different colored dots inside a solid colored grid in the example. Count the number of different colored dots. Next you will make a single column one square in width and X squares in height where X is the number of colored dots you counted. So if there were three dots, it will be 1 block wide by 3 blocks high. Color the column completely red

The input contains a number of colored squares within a larger different-colored square, so count the total number of small colored squares and note that number. Then create a grid that is the noted number of cells high and one cell wide. Color all cells of that new grid red.

Begin by counting the number of squares in the test input grid that are a different colour. This number is the height for your new grid. The width needs to read 1. The colour of the new grid should be all red.

Ignore the predominant colour. Take the boxes that are left and put them in a grid that is the same size in one vertical line and make them all red. (i.e 4 boxes of different colours and put them in a 4 height, 1 width grid)

change the dimension of the grid to 2 by 1 and fill the squares red. the aim is to see how many squares in the initial grid are a different colour to the rest and this determines the height of the grid, the width remains at 1
resize the grid to 2 by 1 and fill the squares red. the solution for the problem is to match the height of the grid to the number of different coloured blocks within the bigger colour block
Create a blank black grid one square high with a width corresponding to the total number of objects of each color depicted on the screen.

Set the grid height to one square.  The grid width should equal to the number of shapes in the test.  For example, it there are three shapes, the width should be 3 squares.  The squares should be black.

Consider the perimeter of all the shapes displayed so that the shapes closest to the edges form a square.  Resize the grid 1 row.  For the number of squares across look at the first number of black squares from left to right until a vertical line or shape is reached.  All squares in the solution should be black.
Make a grid by using the amount of different colours used in the grid
Count the number of objects. Change the grid size to be 1 H by number of objects wide. Leave grid black.
I follow the task demonstration
Count the number of shapes (some may overlap but 1 colour should be counted as 1 shape). Resize the grid so the number of columns is equal to the number of shapes and the number of rows is 1.
count the number of different shapes. make the input grid the same size as the number of unique shapes. 1 shape = 1x1 grid etc.
Count how many different shapes are in the input. Then make a horizontal black line with width being the number of shapes
Count the number of shapes in your input. Resize the output such that it has a height of 1, and the width is the number of shapes in the output. Make all of these squares black. That is your output.
